speaker: interviewee
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: So Eddie, how about introducing your family to us?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. This is my wife, Gloria. How long have we been married? [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: A few weeks.

speaker: interviewee
text: A few weeks. It's going to be almost 50 years, thank God.

speaker: interviewee
text: 48 years in a couple weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wow.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is my daughter, Leah. And I have another child, and that's Jay. He's in the middle. And my daughter-in-law, Diane. And my granddaughter, the only one I have, is Danielle. And that's my family. It's not a big one. But they'll keep it going, I hope.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Anything that you want to say? Let me ask your mother first, OK? Gloria, anything you want to say? What was it like living with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: What was it like?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think the gray hair came from problems. I think it was family genes or something. It has not been bad at all. It's like every family. There is good and there is bad. And we're still together.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And Leah?

speaker: interviewee
text: What was it like?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't talk at all about the Holocaust when I was growing up. I would ask questions, because I was very curious. But he was very reticent to talk about it. And I would read a lot of stories about it. And once in awhile, I would ask him a question that he would answer. Or he would tell me to ask my mother and get the answer from her.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you spoke to your mother more, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, you did. Yes, yes. Anything else you want to say? I believe you were responsible for getting him to Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. It was a few years ago. I finally decided it was time to, to get that connection that had been missing all these years. So I asked him as a favor for me to go and, and see if we can recapture some of, some of those lost years for me, not for him. And I think it ended up for both of us, actually.

speaker: interviewer
text: That somehow the Holocaust was alive for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. But I needed answers. I needed lots of-- I had so many questions that he couldn't answer until we went there, until he was ready to actually face it again.

speaker: interviewer
text: And all three of you went?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, all three of us went.

speaker: interviewee
text: And even now, there are times when he can talk. He's always talked more to me. But it was only at certain times. And if you asked a question, very often, the answer would be, I don't know, I don't remember. And you knew there was no sense going further. Because he just couldn't. And it wasn't a matter of 'wouldn't.' It was 'couldn't.'

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm.

speaker: interviewee
text: I told you before that the reason Jay, my son, didn't go is-- you asked her just now. And I think he's just too soft. I think he couldn't take it. If he sees this tape, he's going to kill me. But I think that's the truth. I think he's just-- every time we start talking now, he just backs out, or goes away and do something.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean by too soft?

speaker: interviewee
text: He's a soft-hearted kid. He just couldn't--

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean, to see what his father went through. That that would be very painful.

speaker: interviewee
text: So is she. But she tries to hide it. She compensates by asking question. If she didn't, she'd be crying all the time. So she compensated by, by asking and me having to answer.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it doesn't work out bad. But she tries to give you the thing that 'I'm not a softie.' She's pretty much the same way as her brother is. You wanted to reply? [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't see it as an issue of soft or hard. For me there was-- I'm very introspective. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I really felt the need to connect. It was a part of-- it's a part of our legacy. And I needed to know what that legacy was about and shed many, many tears, obviously.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you for coming.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thanks. I will-- I have one comment to make before I make-- show those pictures. And that is I am sorry, to a point, that Goebbels and Hitler are not here to see what happened. That we are all alive and living. Thank you. Now in this picture that you see now is my son, Jay, and my daughter-in-law, Diane. By the way, her parents are also survivors. They come from-- one from Hungary and one from someplace else. And my granddaughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us which, which is which.

speaker: interviewee
text: My, my daughter-in-law is in the orange outfit. And my only granddaughter I have, and she is the other one. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is Danielle.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's you daughter-in-law's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Diane. And Danielle is now in college. So Mr. Hitler, eat your heart out.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken, I think, in 1992 or '93.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where, Eddie?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1990, I guess.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was done at a Jewish wedding, Mr. Hitler.

speaker: interviewer
text: In which city?

speaker: interviewee
text: Here in LA.

speaker: interviewer
text: In LA, California.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, LA, Los Angeles, California.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank-- And this is a picture of myself and my wife, in 1947. As you can see, I'm a little skinnier than I am now. And this was done on the corner of Adams and La Brea.

speaker: interviewer
text: In?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the city of Los Angeles, California.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh! That's you on the, on the right, there! [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is a picture of our wedding in 1947. My mother-in-law on one side, my aunt on the other side.

speaker: interviewer
text: Say left to right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the people behind are from the Zionist organization called Habonim, who ended up in Israel. Most of them are still there. And my mother-in-law is on the left, next to my wife. And my cousin, like I told you before in the interview, is on my left.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which cousin is that, Eddie?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the only one I had over here in LA.

speaker: interviewer
text: [NON-ENGLISH] daughter?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And when-- where was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In LA. What was that place called? The-- we had the wedding in--

speaker: interviewer
text: Los Angeles is good enough.

speaker: interviewee
text: Can't remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: Park Manor Hotel.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's it.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, Parkview Manor.

speaker: interviewee
text: Parkview Manor.

speaker: interviewee
text: Parkview Manor.

speaker: interviewee
text: Parkview Manor on 7th and Alvarado.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what date?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a long time ago.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is a picture of my son-in-law and my daughter. My son-in-law, Al Horrocks, and my daughter, Leah, in this picture. It was taken in 1982, in the state of California, in the city of Los Angeles.
